mappings.tier3_evidence_ref: concept_note.md (Pillar 1 — Neuro-Symbolic Planning and Reasoning section; Pillar 2 — Adaptive Memory Architecture section; Cross-Cutting Capability — External Tool and API Orchestration Layer section; Vision: 'robust, trustworthy, and deployable in real-world settings'); project_summary.json (abstract: 'next-generation AI agent architectures that combine structured planning, persistent memory management, robust multi-step reasoning'); strategic_positioning.md (Positioning Against the Expected Outcomes, EO-01 paragraph: 'MAESTRO addresses this through Pillars 1 and 2'; Risks Addressed: 'validation in operational environments at TRL 5')
mappings.mapping_description: MAESTRO directly addresses this expected outcome through two integrated research pillars unified by a cross-cutting orchestration capability. Pillar 1 (Neuro-Symbolic Planning and Reasoning) develops a hybrid planning engine combining LLM-based reasoning with formal symbolic planning methods, providing verifiable sub-task decomposition with formally specified pre-conditions, post-conditions, and verification checkpoints; integration of search-based planning algorithms (Monte Carlo Tree Search, hierarchical task networks) with LLM-driven heuristic evaluation; and self-correction through plan monitoring that detects execution failures and replans from verified intermediate states — directly advancing agent robustness and reliability over long task horizons. Pillar 2 (Adaptive Memory Architecture) develops working memory (structured short-term store for current goals and active plan state), episodic memory (retrieval-augmented store of past task episodes), and semantic memory (persistent knowledge graph), managed by a memory controller that balances recall accuracy against computational cost — directly addressing memory management to sustain coherent long-horizon agent behaviour. The External Tool and API Orchestration Layer enhances agent reliability by grounding reasoning in verified external data through post-invocation validation and providing principled fallback/recovery mechanisms for tool failures. Together these pillars and the orchestration layer produce significant improvements in AI agent autonomy, robustness, and reliability through the specific planning mechanisms, memory management, and reasoning capabilities named in EO-01.

mappings.tier3_evidence_ref: concept_note.md (Pillar 3 — Decentralised Multi-Agent Coordination section: 'Many real-world tasks exceed the scope of any single agent'; formal task-delegation protocol with provable convergence; conflict resolution mechanisms; auditable traces; Advanced Manufacturing demonstrator: 'Agents coordinate across production line segments'); project_summary.json (abstract: 'decentralised multi-agent coordination protocols that enable heterogeneous AI agents to negotiate, delegate, and jointly execute tasks with provable convergence guarantees'); strategic_positioning.md (Positioning Against the Expected Outcomes, EO-02 paragraph: 'formally specified delegation and negotiation protocols with convergence guarantees'; Positioning Within the Apply AI Sectors, Advanced Manufacturing paragraph)
mappings.mapping_description: MAESTRO directly addresses this expected outcome through Pillar 3 (Decentralised Multi-Agent Coordination). The concept develops multi-agent coordination protocols enabling heterogeneous agents to collaborate on shared objectives, including: a formal task-delegation protocol where agents decompose tasks, negotiate subtask assignments, and establish shared commitment structures with provable convergence; conflict resolution mechanisms for situations where agent plans interfere, resources are contested, or partial observations lead to inconsistent world models; and auditable structured delegation traces that humans can inspect. The concept explicitly frames this as going beyond single-agent capability: 'Many real-world tasks exceed the scope of any single agent.' The advanced manufacturing demonstrator deploys a multi-agent system coordinating agents across production line segments in real time, and the logistics demonstrator demonstrates agents negotiating across distributed supply chain actors — both explicitly exercising multi-agent capabilities beyond individual agent scope. The External Tool and API Orchestration Layer extends multi-agent collaboration by governing shared tool access across collaborating agents and resolving conflicts when multiple agents require the same external resource.
